title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice on a website:

'We want reviews of books that have had a significant impact on you. Write a review of a book you have read recently, describing what it is about and explaining why you would or would not recommend it to other readers.'

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Book Review: The Alchemist by Paulo Coelho

The Alchemist is a philosophical novel by Brazilian author Paulo Coelho, first published in Portuguese in 1988. It tells the story of Santiago, a young Andalusian shepherd who embarks on a journey to find a treasure buried near the Egyptian pyramids. Along the way, he encounters a series of characters who teach him profound lessons about following one's dreams.

What makes this book so remarkable is its simplicity. The writing is clear and accessible, making it ideal for English learners, yet the ideas it explores — about destiny, courage, and listening to your heart — are genuinely thought-provoking. I found myself pausing frequently to reflect on my own choices and ambitions.

Some readers may find the story overly simple or idealistic. However, I believe that is precisely its strength. Sometimes the most powerful messages are the most straightforward ones.

This is a book that stays with you long after the final page. I recommend it unreservedly. Rating: 5/5

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'In the age of smartphones and streaming services, is reading books becoming a lost habit? Write an article discussing whether reading is still important and how we can encourage more people, especially young people, to read for pleasure.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Page Turns On: Why Reading Still Matters

In an era of TikTok, Netflix, and endless scrolling, sitting down with a book can feel almost revolutionary. Yet reading remains one of the most powerful and rewarding activities a person can engage in — and we should be doing everything we can to keep it alive.

Reading builds vocabulary, improves writing skills, and enhances empathy by allowing us to experience lives very different from our own. Unlike watching a film, reading requires and develops concentration — a skill increasingly under threat from the constant distraction of digital devices.

So how can we encourage more young people to read? Libraries need investment to become vibrant, welcoming spaces rather than dusty afterthoughts. Schools should allow students to choose books that genuinely interest them rather than forcing everyone to read the same classics. And social media can actually help — online book communities like BookTok have introduced millions of young readers to new titles.

Reading is not dying. It is waiting to be rediscovered.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Morgan runs a small book club and has invited you to join. Write an email to Morgan saying whether you would like to join, asking about the types of books the club reads, and recommending a book you think the group would enjoy.

Write your email to Morgan.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Morgan,

Thank you so much for the invitation — joining your book club sounds wonderful! I've been looking for a way to read more regularly, and discussing books with others would make the experience even more enjoyable. Count me in!

Before the first meeting, could you tell me a bit more about what kinds of books the club tends to choose? I'm happy to read most genres, but I have a particular love of literary fiction and historical novels. I'd also like to know how often you meet and whether everyone is expected to finish the book before each session.

On the subject of recommendations, I'd love to suggest a book called The God of Small Things by Arundhati Roy. It's a beautiful, lyrical novel set in India that deals with themes of love, family, and social injustice. The writing is absolutely stunning, and I think it would generate a really interesting discussion.

Looking forward to meeting everyone!

Warm regards,
Ida
